expanded_description: Understanding the three fundamental molecular geometries—linear, trigonal planar, and tetrahedral—that form the foundation of VSEPR theory, including their bond angles, electron domain arrangements, and examples of molecules that adopt each geometry.

core_concept.principle: Linear ($180°$), trigonal planar ($120°$), and tetrahedral ($109.5°$) are the three fundamental molecular geometries that arise from steric numbers 2, 3, and 4 respectively, forming the basis for predicting molecular shapes using VSEPR theory.
core_concept.explanation: These three geometries represent the most efficient arrangements for 2, 3, and 4 electron domains around a central atom. Linear geometry maximizes separation for 2 domains (opposite sides), trigonal planar for 3 domains (corners of equilateral triangle), and tetrahedral for 4 domains (corners of tetrahedron). Understanding these fundamental geometries is essential because all other molecular shapes are derived from them by replacing bonding pairs with lone pairs.

image.alt: Diagram showing the three fundamental molecular geometries: linear (180°), trigonal planar (120°), and tetrahedral (109.5°)
image.caption: Three fundamental geometries: linear (SN=2, 180°), trigonal planar (SN=3, 120°), tetrahedral (SN=4, 109.5°)

sections.introduction: Linear geometry is the simplest molecular geometry, occurring when a central atom has exactly 2 electron domains. The electron domains arrange themselves on opposite sides of the central atom to maximize separation, resulting in a bond angle of $180°$.

key_concept.definition: Linear geometry occurs when a central atom has steric number 2 (2 bonding domains, 0 lone pairs), resulting in a straight-line arrangement with $180°$ bond angles.
key_concept.context: This is the only geometry possible for steric number 2; there are no lone pair variations because 2 electron domains must both be bonding pairs to form a molecule.

principles.statement: Two electron domains achieve maximum separation by positioning themselves on opposite sides of the central atom, creating a $180°$ bond angle.
principles.application: Predicts linear geometry for any molecule with steric number 2; explains why bond angle is exactly $180°$.
principles.example: CO₂: O=C=O has 2 double bonds = 2 electron domains → linear geometry with $180°$ bond angle
principles.analogy: Like two people standing on opposite sides of a pole—they maximize distance by being $180°$ apart.

principles.statement: Multiple bonds (double, triple) count as single electron domains for geometry prediction but exert greater repulsion than single bonds.
principles.application: Explains why CO₂ and HCN are both linear despite different bond types; each bonding region counts as one domain.
principles.example: CO₂: 2 double bonds = 2 domains; HCN: 1 single + 1 triple bond = 2 domains; both linear
principles.analogy: Like counting rooms in a house: a large room (double bond) and small room (single bond) each count as one room.

principles.statement: Steric number 2 always results in linear molecular geometry because both electron domains must be bonding pairs.
principles.application: Simplifies prediction: SN = 2 always means linear; no need to consider lone pair effects.
principles.example: All SN = 2 molecules (CO₂, BeCl₂, HCN, C₂H₂) are linear regardless of central atom identity
principles.analogy: Like a two-seat car: both seats must be occupied by passengers (bonding pairs); no empty seats (lone pairs) possible.

classifications.value: Central atom forms 2 double bonds with surrounding atoms
characteristics: Central atom typically from Group 14 (C, Si)
No lone pairs on central atom
classifications.behavior: Double bonds count as single electron domains; molecule is linear with $180°$ bond angle

examples.explanation: C central atom with 2 double bonds to O; 2 electron domains; linear; $180°$ bond angle; nonpolar molecule
C central atom with 2 double bonds to S; 2 electron domains; linear; $180°$ bond angle

classifications.value: Central atom forms 1 single bond and 1 triple bond with surrounding atoms
characteristics: No lone pairs on central atom
May be polar if surrounding atoms different
classifications.behavior: Both bonding regions count as single electron domains; molecule is linear with $180°$ bond angle

examples.explanation: C central atom with 1 single bond to H + 1 triple bond to N; 2 electron domains; linear; $180°$ bond angle; polar molecule
Each C has 1 single bond to H + 1 triple bond to other C; 2 electron domains per C; linear; $180°$ bond angle

classifications.value: Central atom from Group 2 forms 2 single bonds with surrounding atoms
characteristics: No lone pairs on central atom
classifications.behavior: 2 single bonds = 2 electron domains; molecule is linear with $180°$ bond angle

examples.explanation: Be central atom with 2 single bonds to Cl; 2 electron domains; linear; $180°$ bond angle; Be has incomplete octet
Be central atom with 2 single bonds to H; 2 electron domains; linear; $180°$ bond angle

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for CO₂
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angle for carbon dioxide (CO₂).

step_by_step.explanation: C is central atom with 4 valence electrons; forms 2 double bonds with O atoms; no lone pairs on C
step_by_step.calculation: O=C=O; C has 4 bonds, 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 2 double bonds = 2 electron domains (each bonding region counts as 1 domain)
2 electron domains → linear electron domain geometry
0 lone pairs → molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry
Linear geometry has exactly $180°$ bond angle

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂: Steric number = 2; Electron domain geometry = linear; Molecular geometry = linear; Bond angle = $180°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 2 always gives linear geometry; multiple bonds count as single electron domains.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for HCN
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for hydrogen cyanide (HCN).

step_by_step.explanation: C is central atom with 4 valence electrons; forms 1 single bond with H + 1 triple bond with N; no lone pairs on C
step_by_step.calculation: H-C≡N; C has 4 bonds, 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 1 single bond + 1 triple bond = 2 electron domains
2 electron domains → linear; 0 lone pairs → molecular geometry = linear
Linear geometry has $180°$ bond angle

worked_examples.final_answer: HCN: Steric number = 2; Molecular geometry = linear; Bond angle = $180°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Different bond types (single + triple) still count as 2 electron domains; geometry is linear.

sections.key_concept: Trigonal planar geometry occurs when a central atom has steric number 3 with 0 lone pairs. The three electron domains arrange themselves at the corners of an equilateral triangle in a single plane, with bond angles of $120°$.

categories.description: Central atom has 3 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs, resulting in trigonal planar molecular geometry.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Three electron domains achieve maximum separation by arranging at $120°$ angles in a single plane; all positions equivalent.

examples.explanation: B central atom with 3 single bonds to F; 3 electron domains; 0 lone pairs; trigonal planar; $120°$ bond angles
S central atom with 3 double bonds to O (resonance); 3 electron domains; 0 lone pairs; trigonal planar; $120°$ bond angles
C central atom with 3 bonding regions (1 double + 2 single, resonance); 3 electron domains; 0 lone pairs; trigonal planar; $120°$ bond angles

categories.description: Central atom has 2 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair, resulting in bent molecular geometry derived from trigonal planar electron domain geometry.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pair occupies one of three trigonal planar positions; lone pair-bonding pair repulsion compresses bond angle below $120°$.

examples.explanation: S central atom with 2 bonding regions + 1 lone pair; 3 electron domains; bent molecular geometry; bond angle < $120°$ (~$119°$)
Central O with 2 bonding regions + 1 lone pair; 3 electron domains; bent molecular geometry; bond angle ~$117°$

categories.description: Bond angles in trigonal planar family vary based on lone pair presence and atom electronegativity.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing bond angles; more electronegative surrounding atoms pull bonding electrons away, reducing bonding pair repulsion.

examples.explanation: BF₃ (0 LP): $120°$; SO₂ (1 LP): ~$119°$; lone pair compresses angle slightly
More electronegative surrounding atoms → bonding pairs pulled away from central atom → less BP-BP repulsion → slightly smaller angles

classifications.value: Trigonal planar electron domain geometry for steric number 3
characteristics: Basis for both trigonal planar and bent molecular geometries
classifications.behavior: Electron domain geometry is always trigonal planar for SN = 3; molecular geometry depends on lone pair count

examples.explanation: 3 BP, 0 LP → electron domain = trigonal planar; molecular geometry = trigonal planar
2 BP, 1 LP → electron domain = trigonal planar; molecular geometry = bent

classifications.value: Two molecular geometries possible for steric number 3
characteristics: 2 lone pairs not possible for SN = 3 (would require only 1 bonding pair)
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs reduce bond angles from ideal $120°$; molecular geometry name based on atom positions only

examples.explanation: BF₃, SO₃, CO₃²⁻, NO₃⁻ (all 0 lone pairs, $120°$ angles)
SO₂, O₃, NO₂⁻ (all 1 lone pair, < $120°$ angles)

classifications.value: Trigonal planar molecules may be polar or nonpolar depending on symmetry
classifications.behavior: Molecular polarity affects physical properties like boiling point and solubility

examples.explanation: Trigonal planar, symmetrical, 3 identical B-F bonds → bond dipoles cancel → nonpolar
Bent, asymmetrical, lone pair → bond dipoles don't cancel → polar molecule

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for BF₃
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for boron trifluoride (BF₃).

step_by_step.explanation: B is central atom with 3 valence electrons; forms 3 single bonds with F atoms; B has incomplete octet (6 electrons); no lone pairs on B
step_by_step.calculation: B: 3 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 3 electron domains
3 electron domains → trigonal planar electron domain geometry
0 lone pairs → molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry
Trigonal planar has ideal $120°$ bond angles; no lone pairs to compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: BF₃: Steric number = 3; Electron domain geometry = trigonal planar; Molecular geometry = trigonal planar; Bond angles = $120°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 3 with 0 lone pairs gives trigonal planar molecular geometry with $120°$ bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for SO₂
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angle for sulfur dioxide (SO₂).

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms bonds with 2 O atoms; S has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 2 bonds use 4 e⁻ (with resonance); 2 e⁻ remain as 1 lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding regions + 1 lone pair = 3 electron domains
3 electron domains → trigonal planar electron domain geometry
1 lone pair → molecular geometry is bent (atom positions only)
1 lone pair compresses angle from ideal $120°$ to ~$119°$

worked_examples.final_answer: SO₂: Steric number = 3; Electron domain geometry = trigonal planar; Molecular geometry = bent; Bond angle < $120°$ (~$119°$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 3 with 1 lone pair gives bent molecular geometry; lone pair compresses bond angle below $120°$.

sections.key_concept: Tetrahedral geometry occurs when a central atom has steric number 4 with 0 lone pairs. The four electron domains arrange themselves at the corners of a tetrahedron with bond angles of $109.5°$, representing the most efficient three-dimensional arrangement for four electron domains.

components.definition: Tetrahedral geometry has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs around the central atom, with bond angles of $109.5°$; this is the most common geometry for main group elements.
components.interpretation: Tetrahedral arrangement maximizes separation of 4 electron domains in three-dimensional space; lone pairs compress bond angles below $109.5°$.

image.alt: Diagram showing tetrahedral geometry with central atom and 4 surrounding atoms at 109.5° angles
image.caption: Tetrahedral geometry: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs, $109.5°$ bond angles (CH₄ shown)

principles.statement: Four electron domains achieve maximum separation by arranging at the corners of a tetrahedron with $109.5°$ bond angles.
principles.application: Predicts tetrahedral geometry for steric number 4; explains why bond angles are $109.5°$ not $90°$ or $120°$.
principles.example: CH₄: 4 C-H bonds arrange tetrahedrally with $109.5°$ angles; most stable arrangement
principles.analogy: Like 4 balloons tied together at a central point—they naturally form a tetrahedron to maximize separation.

principles.statement: Each lone pair on a tetrahedral central atom compresses bond angles by approximately $2-2.5°$ from the ideal $109.5°$.
principles.application: Explains bond angle variations in tetrahedral family: CH₄ ($109.5°$) > NH₃ ($107°$) > H₂O ($104.5°$).
principles.example: H₂O has 2 lone pairs; bond angle compressed from $109.5°$ to $104.5°$ (reduction of ~$5°$)
principles.analogy: Like large people (lone pairs) at a table taking up more space, pushing others (bonding pairs) closer together.

principles.statement: Steric number 4 produces three molecular geometries depending on lone pair count: tetrahedral (0 LP), trigonal pyramidal (1 LP), and bent (2 LP).
principles.application: All three geometries share tetrahedral electron domain geometry; molecular geometry depends on atom positions only.
principles.example: CH₄ (0 LP): tetrahedral; NH₃ (1 LP): trigonal pyramidal; H₂O (2 LP): bent; all SN = 4
principles.analogy: Like a family with same parents (electron domain geometry) but different children (molecular geometries).

classifications.value: Central atom has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs
characteristics: Nonpolar if all surrounding atoms identical
Most common geometry for carbon compounds
classifications.behavior: All bond angles equal $109.5°$; molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry

examples.explanation: C central atom with 4 single bonds to H; 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; $109.5°$ bond angles; nonpolar
C central atom with 4 single bonds to Cl; 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; $109.5°$ bond angles; nonpolar
N central atom with 4 single bonds to H; 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; $109.5°$ bond angles; positive charge

classifications.value: Central atom has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair
characteristics: Lone pair occupies one tetrahedral position
Molecular geometry based on 3 atoms only
classifications.behavior: Lone pair compresses bond angles; molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal (3 atoms form pyramid base)

examples.explanation: N central atom with 3 single bonds to H + 1 lone pair; 3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; ~$107°$ bond angles; polar
P central atom with 3 single bonds to H + 1 lone pair; 3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; ~$93°$ bond angles (P less electronegative)
O central atom with 3 single bonds to H + 1 lone pair; 3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; ~$107°$ bond angles; positive charge

classifications.value: Central atom has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs
characteristics: Molecular geometry based on 2 atoms only
classifications.behavior: 2 lone pairs compress bond angles more than 1 lone pair; molecular geometry is bent (V-shaped)

examples.explanation: O central atom with 2 single bonds to H + 2 lone pairs; 2 BP, 2 LP; bent; $104.5°$ bond angles; polar
S central atom with 2 single bonds to H + 2 lone pairs; 2 BP, 2 LP; bent; ~$92°$ bond angles (S less electronegative than O)
O central atom with 2 single bonds to F + 2 lone pairs; 2 BP, 2 LP; bent; ~$103°$ bond angles

classifications.type: Bond Angle Trend in Tetrahedral Family
classifications.value: Bond angles systematically decrease as lone pair count increases
characteristics: Each lone pair compresses angles by ~$2-2.5°$
classifications.behavior: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion > lone pair-bonding pair repulsion > bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion

examples.explanation: CH₄ ($109.5°$) > NH₃ ($107°$) > H₂O ($104.5°$); progressive angle compression with more lone pairs

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for CH₄
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for methane (CH₄).

step_by_step.explanation: C is central atom with 4 valence electrons; forms 4 single bonds with H atoms; no lone pairs on C
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 valence e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 4 electron domains
4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
0 lone pairs → molecular geometry same as electron domain geometry
Tetrahedral has ideal $109.5°$ bond angles; no lone pairs to compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: CH₄: Steric number = 4; Electron domain geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = tetrahedral; Bond angles = $109.5°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 4 with 0 lone pairs gives tetrahedral molecular geometry with $109.5°$ bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for NH₃
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for ammonia (NH₃).

step_by_step.explanation: N is central atom with 5 valence electrons; forms 3 single bonds with H atoms; N has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: N: 5 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 4 electron domains
4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
1 lone pair → molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal (3 atoms only)
1 lone pair compresses angles from $109.5°$ to ~$107°$

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₃: Steric number = 4; Electron domain geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal; Bond angles ≈ $107°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 4 with 1 lone pair gives trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry; lone pair compresses bond angles from $109.5°$ to ~$107°$.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for H₂O
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for water (H₂O).

step_by_step.explanation: O is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 2 single bonds with H atoms; O has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: O: 6 valence e⁻; 2 bonds use 2 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 4 electron domains
4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
2 lone pairs → molecular geometry is bent (2 atoms only)
2 lone pairs compress angles from $109.5°$ to ~$104.5°$ (greater compression than NH₃)

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O: Steric number = 4; Electron domain geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = bent; Bond angles ≈ $104.5°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 4 with 2 lone pairs gives bent molecular geometry; 2 lone pairs compress bond angles more than 1 lone pair ($104.5°$ vs. $107°$).

sections.key_concept: Understanding the relationships and differences between linear, trigonal planar, and tetrahedral geometries enables systematic prediction of molecular shapes and properties.

process_flow.description: Count bonding pairs + lone pairs on central atom from Lewis structure.
SN = 2 → linear; SN = 3 → trigonal planar; SN = 4 → tetrahedral.
Determine number of lone pairs on central atom.
process_flow.result: Number of lone pairs (0 to SN-2)

process_flow.description: Based on atom positions only; lone pairs influence shape but not named.
Start with ideal angles ($180°$, $120°$, $109.5°$); adjust for lone pair compression.

classifications.value: Each steric number corresponds to specific electron domain geometry
characteristics: Higher SN = smaller ideal bond angles (more domains to separate)
classifications.behavior: Steric number determines electron domain geometry; lone pairs determine molecular geometry variations

examples.explanation: CO₂ (SN=2): linear $180°$; BF₃ (SN=3): trigonal planar $120°$; CH₄ (SN=4): tetrahedral $109.5°$

classifications.value: Lone pairs compress bond angles in all three geometries
characteristics: SN = 2: no lone pair variations possible
classifications.behavior: More lone pairs = greater angle compression; LP-LP repulsion strongest

examples.explanation: CH₄ ($109.5°$) > NH₃ ($107°$) > H₂O ($104.5°$); progressive compression with more lone pairs

classifications.value: Number of molecular geometries increases with steric number
characteristics: SN = 4: 3 molecular geometries (tetrahedral, trigonal pyramidal, bent)
classifications.behavior: More electron domains = more possible lone pair combinations = more molecular geometry variations

examples.explanation: SN=2: 1 geometry; SN=3: 2 geometries; SN=4: 3 geometries

classifications.value: Molecular polarity depends on geometry and surrounding atom identity
characteristics: Linear: nonpolar if symmetrical (CO₂), polar if asymmetrical (HCN)
Trigonal planar: nonpolar if symmetrical (BF₃), polar if asymmetrical or with lone pairs (SO₂)
Tetrahedral: nonpolar if symmetrical (CH₄), polar if with lone pairs (NH₃, H₂O)
classifications.behavior: Symmetrical geometries with identical surrounding atoms are nonpolar; lone pairs often create polarity

examples.explanation: CO₂ (linear, symmetrical), BF₃ (trigonal planar, symmetrical), CH₄ (tetrahedral, symmetrical)
HCN (linear, asymmetrical), SO₂ (bent, lone pair), NH₃ (trigonal pyramidal, lone pair), H₂O (bent, lone pairs)

worked_examples.title: Comparing CO₂, BF₃, and CH₄
worked_examples.problem: Compare the molecular geometries and bond angles of CO₂, BF₃, and CH₄.

step_by_step.explanation: C central atom; 2 double bonds = 2 electron domains; 0 lone pairs
B central atom; 3 single bonds = 3 electron domains; 0 lone pairs
C central atom; 4 single bonds = 4 electron domains; 0 lone pairs
More electron domains = smaller bond angles (more domains to separate in 3D space)
Steric number determines electron domain geometry and ideal bond angles

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂: linear, $180°$ (SN=2); BF₃: trigonal planar, $120°$ (SN=3); CH₄: tetrahedral, $109.5°$ (SN=4); bond angles decrease as steric number increases
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number determines electron domain geometry; more electron domains require smaller bond angles for maximum separation.

worked_examples.title: Comparing CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O
worked_examples.problem: Compare the molecular geometries and bond angles of CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O. All have steric number 4.

step_by_step.explanation: C central atom; 4 BP, 0 LP; SN = 4
N central atom; 3 BP, 1 LP; SN = 4
O central atom; 2 BP, 2 LP; SN = 4
More lone pairs = greater bond angle compression from ideal $109.5°$
Same steric number but different lone pair counts give different molecular geometries and bond angles
step_by_step.calculation: Lone pairs compress bond angles; more lone pairs = more compression

worked_examples.final_answer: CH₄: tetrahedral, $109.5°$ (0 LP); NH₃: trigonal pyramidal, ~$107°$ (1 LP); H₂O: bent, ~$104.5°$ (2 LP); lone pairs compress bond angles progressively
worked_examples.concept_applied: Same steric number with different lone pair counts produces different molecular geometries; lone pairs compress bond angles from ideal values.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about these three fundamental geometries can lead to incorrect predictions. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of molecular shape prediction.

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Electron Domain and Molecular Geometry
classifications.value: Using electron domain geometry name when molecular geometry is different due to lone pairs
characteristics: Calling NH₃ tetrahedral instead of trigonal pyramidal
Calling H₂O tetrahedral instead of bent
Not distinguishing between electron pair arrangement and atom arrangement
classifications.behavior: Remember: electron domain geometry includes lone pairs; molecular geometry names only atom positions

examples.explanation: Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal
Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = bent

classifications.value: Counting each bond in a multiple bond as separate electron domains
classifications.behavior: Remember: each bonding region (single, double, or triple bond) counts as ONE electron domain

examples.explanation: Wrong: 4 domains (counting each bond in double bonds); Correct: 2 domains (2 double bonds = 2 domains)
Wrong: 4 domains; Correct: 2 domains (1 single + 1 triple = 2 domains)

classifications.type: Error: Assuming Bond Angles Always Equal Ideal Values
classifications.value: Predicting $109.5°$ for all SN = 4 molecules regardless of lone pairs
characteristics: Not accounting for lone pair compression
classifications.behavior: Remember: lone pairs compress bond angles; 1 LP ≈ $2-2.5°$ compression; 2 LP ≈ $5°$ compression

examples.explanation: Wrong: $109.5°$; Correct: ~$107°$ (1 lone pair compression)
Wrong: $109.5°$; Correct: ~$104.5°$ (2 lone pair compression)

classifications.type: Error: Forgetting Linear Has No Lone Pair Variations
classifications.value: Thinking linear geometry can have lone pair variations like trigonal planar and tetrahedral
characteristics: Assuming lone pairs possible for SN = 2
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 2 always means linear molecular geometry; both electron domains must be bonding pairs

examples.explanation: All SN = 2 molecules (CO₂, BeCl₂, HCN) are linear; no bent variations possible

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Trigonal Planar and Trigonal Pyramidal
classifications.value: Mixing up trigonal planar (SN = 3, 0 LP) with trigonal pyramidal (SN = 4, 1 LP)
classifications.behavior: Remember: trigonal planar = SN = 3, flat, $120°$; trigonal pyramidal = SN = 4, 3D pyramid, ~$107°$

examples.explanation: BF₃: trigonal planar (SN=3, $120°$); NH₃: trigonal pyramidal (SN=4, ~$107°$)

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Linear, Trigonal Planar, and Tetrahedral Geometries
sections.introduction: Test your understanding of the three fundamental molecular geometries with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of steric number determination, geometry prediction, and lone pair effects on bond angles.
key_concept.context: Focus on systematic approach: Lewis structure → electron domains → electron domain geometry → molecular geometry → bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angle for BeCl₂.

step_by_step.explanation: Be is central atom with 2 valence electrons; forms 2 single bonds with Cl atoms; Be has incomplete octet; 0 lone pairs
step_by_step.calculation: Be: 2 valence e⁻; 2 bonds use 2 e⁻; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 0 lone pairs = 2 electron domains
SN = 2 → linear; 0 lone pairs → molecular geometry = linear
Linear geometry has $180°$ bond angle

worked_examples.final_answer: BeCl₂: Steric number = 2; Molecular geometry = linear; Bond angle = $180°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 2 always gives linear geometry; Group 2 elements can have incomplete octets.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angle for SO₃.

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 3 bonds with O atoms (with resonance); 0 lone pairs on S
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 3 bonding regions; 0 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding regions + 0 lone pairs = 3 electron domains
SN = 3 → trigonal planar; 0 lone pairs → molecular geometry = trigonal planar
Trigonal planar has $120°$ bond angles; no lone pairs to compress

worked_examples.final_answer: SO₃: Steric number = 3; Molecular geometry = trigonal planar; Bond angles = $120°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 3 with 0 lone pairs gives trigonal planar geometry with $120°$ bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Which has larger bond angles: CH₄ or H₂O? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Both have 4 electron domains → SN = 4 → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
CH₄: 0 lone pairs; H₂O: 2 lone pairs
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: 4 BP, 0 LP; H₂O: 2 BP, 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: Lone pairs compress bond angles; more lone pairs = greater compression
CH₄ has no lone pairs; H₂O has 2 lone pairs compressing angles

worked_examples.final_answer: CH₄ has larger bond angles ($109.5°$) than H₂O (~$104.5°$) because H₂O has 2 lone pairs that compress bond angles
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs compress bond angles; molecules with more lone pairs have smaller bond angles for same steric number.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for the nitrate ion (NO₃⁻).

step_by_step.explanation: N is central atom with 5 valence electrons; -1 charge means 1 extra electron; forms 3 bonds with O atoms (with resonance); 0 lone pairs on N
step_by_step.calculation: N: 5 valence e⁻ + 1 (charge) = 6 e⁻ for bonding; 3 bonding regions

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding regions (resonance) + 0 lone pairs = 3 electron domains
SN = 3 → trigonal planar; 0 lone pairs → molecular geometry = trigonal planar
Trigonal planar has $120°$ bond angles; resonance makes all bonds equivalent

worked_examples.final_answer: NO₃⁻: Steric number = 3; Molecular geometry = trigonal planar; Bond angles = $120°$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance structures don't change electron domain count; NO₃⁻ has 3 bonding regions = trigonal planar.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Explain why CO₂ is linear but SO₂ is bent, even though both have a central atom bonded to two oxygen atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: C is central with 4 valence electrons; forms 2 double bonds with O; no lone pairs on C
S is central with 6 valence electrons; forms bonds with 2 O; has 1 lone pair on S
CO₂: 2 electron domains; SO₂: 3 electron domains
CO₂: SN = 2 → linear; SO₂: SN = 3 → bent (due to lone pair)
Different central atoms have different valence electron counts, leading to different lone pair counts and geometries
step_by_step.calculation: Central atom identity matters for VSEPR predictions

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂ is linear (SN = 2, 0 LP) because C has no lone pairs; SO₂ is bent (SN = 3, 1 LP) because S has a lone pair that affects geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Central atom valence electron count determines lone pair count; different central atoms with same bonding pattern can have different geometries.

summary: Three fundamental molecular geometries form the foundation of VSEPR theory: (1) Linear (SN = 2): 2 electron domains, $180°$ bond angle, no lone pair variations (CO₂, BeCl₂, HCN); (2) Trigonal planar (SN = 3): 3 electron domains, $120°$ ideal bond angle, 2 molecular geometries (0 LP = trigonal planar, 1 LP = bent < $120°$) (BF₃, SO₂); (3) Tetrahedral (SN = 4): 4 electron domains, $109.5°$ ideal bond angle, 3 molecular geometries (0 LP = tetrahedral $109.5°$, 1 LP = trigonal pyramidal ~$107°$, 2 LP = bent ~$104.5°$) (CH₄, NH₃, H₂O). Key principles: multiple bonds count as single electron domains; lone pairs compress bond angles (LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP repulsion); more lone pairs = greater angle compression. Bond angle trends: SN increases → ideal angles decrease ($180°$ > $120°$ > $109.5°$); lone pairs increase → angles compress further. Common errors include: confusing electron domain and molecular geometry, miscounting electron domains (multiple bonds = 1 domain), assuming bond angles always equal ideal values, forgetting linear has no lone pair variations, and confusing trigonal planar with trigonal pyramidal. Systematic approach: Lewis structure → count electron domains → determine steric number → identify electron domain geometry → count lone pairs → determine molecular geometry → predict bond angles with lone pair adjustments. Mastery of these three fundamental geometries enables prediction of all other molecular shapes derived from them.
